name: Gold
name_origin: The English name comes from the Old English 'geolu' (yellow). The chemical symbol Au is from the Latin 'aurum' meaning 'shining dawn' or 'glow of sunrise'.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Gold is rare in Earth's crust at about 0.004 ppm but is concentrated in hydrothermal quartz veins, placer deposits, and Witwatersrand-type conglomerates. The largest producers today are China, Australia, Russia, the United States, and Canada. Most gold occurs as the native metal, often alloyed with silver as electrum.
states: native gold (often as electrum, Au-Ag alloy)
trace in pyrite and arsenopyrite (refractory ores)

compounds.name: Gold(III) chloride
compounds.summary: A red crystalline solid that is the most common precursor for Au(III) chemistry and homogeneous gold catalysis.

compounds.name: Chloroauric acid (tetrachloroauric acid)
compounds.summary: A yellow-orange water-soluble acid used to electroplate gold and to prepare colloidal gold nanoparticles.

compounds.name: Gold(I) sulfide
compounds.summary: A black sulfide used historically as a pigment and as a precursor in gold colloid chemistry.

compounds.name: Dicyanoaurate(I)
compounds.summary: The water-soluble cyanide complex formed in the cyanide leaching process used to extract gold from low-grade ores.

compounds.name: Gold(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: An orange solid used as a strong fluorinating agent and a probe of relativistic bonding effects in gold chemistry.

uses: Jewelry, decorative arts, and bullion as a store of value
Bonding wires and connector plating in electronics due to corrosion resistance and conductivity
Dentistry (crowns, inlays, bridges) for biocompatibility and durability
Aerospace coatings and visors that reflect infrared radiation
Colloidal gold in lateral-flow diagnostic test strips and electron-microscopy stains
Homogeneous catalysis (Au(I)/Au(III)) for activation of alkynes and alkenes in fine-chemical synthesis
reactivity_summary: Gold is a soft, dense, lustrous yellow metal and the most malleable and ductile of all metals. It is chemically very inert: it does not tarnish in air or water at any temperature, resists most acids, and is dissolved only by aqua regia, selenic acid, hot concentrated H2SO4 with oxidizers, and aqueous halogen or cyanide solutions. The yellow color and anomalous [Xe] 4f14 5d10 6s1 configuration are consequences of relativistic contraction of the 6s orbital.
isotopes_summary: Gold has only one naturally occurring stable isotope, 197Au, with a nuclear spin of 3/2. About 36 radioisotopes have been synthesized, of which 195Au (t1/2 186 d) and 198Au (t1/2 2.7 d) are most important. 198Au has been used in cancer brachytherapy, and Au nanoparticles labeled with 198Au are studied for theranostic applications.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Gold
